statement.en: Enabling and Inhibitory Pathways of University Students' Willingness to Disclose AI Use: A Cognition-Affect-Conation Perspective
statement.summary: The increasing integration of artificial intelligence (AI) in higher education has raised important questions regarding students' transparency in reporting AI-assisted work. This study investigates the psychological mechanisms underlying university students' willingness to disclose AI use by applying the Cognition--Affect--Conation (CAC) framework. A sequential explanatory mixed-methods design was employed. In the quantitative phase, survey data were collected from 546 university students and analysed using structural equation modelling to examine the relationships among cognitive perceptions, affective responses, and disclosure intention. In the qualitative phase, semi-structured interviews with 22 students were conducted to further interpret the quantitative findings. The results indicat

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: This is a preprint from 2026 (future-dated or timestamp error) presenting empirical mixed-methods research on a novel phenomenon (AI disclosure behavior in higher education). The psychological mechanisms proposed are plausible but not yet validated through peer review, replication, or cross-cultural longitudinal studies. The CAC framework application to this domain is provisional—the abstract indicates incomplete reporting (cuts mid-sentence), suggesting manuscript is still in draft/preprint circulation.

ramanujanFormula.interpretation: 認知と感情が同じ方向に働くほど開示意欲は減少する、という逆説的な共鳴。
